entries.content: Opening M57-JEAN investigation. Case question: How did m57plan.xlsx (employee names/salaries/SSNs) get from CFO Jean's M57.biz workstation to a competitor's "technical support" forum, and who within M57.biz was involved? Evidence: single-host disk image (nps-2008-jean.E01/E02), no memory/network capture. Starting phase-entry checklist: hash verification, E01 acquisition metadata, file stats — then mount, pre-plan registry reads, reason.hypothesize on the case question, reason.plan.

entries.conclusion:
ANALYSIS

The headline question is "how did the roster leave Jean's box and who helped." The canonical M57 twist is a spoofed/look-alike recipient, so my first job is to challenge the framing that an insider colluded, and equally to challenge Jean's self-serving "Alison asked me" claim.

Ranked hypotheses:

H1. Social-engineering / look-alike recipient (HIGH)
Jean was tricked into mailing m57plan.xlsx to an address that displays as Alison but whose RFC822 envelope/Return-Path/Received chain points to an external domain (e.g., alison@m75.biz, alex@m57.biz, alison.smith@gmail). Jean is truthful about intent; Alison is truthful about never asking. No M57 insider conspirator required.
Supporting artifacts to seek: Outlook PST Sent Items entry for the xlsx; full MAPI PR_TRANSPORT_MESSAGE_HEADERS; the inbound request email that prompted Jean (Inbox, sender domain, Reply-To mismatch, Received chain); LNK/MRU showing xlsx creation; USN journal showing xlsx written then attached.
Confirms: Sent Items recipient SMTP address ≠ alison@m57.biz, or Received headers on the prompting email show external origin with spoofed display name / Reply-To redirect.
Rules out: Recipient resolves to the genuine alison@m57.biz mailbox on M57 infrastructure and inbound prompt also genuinely from her.

H2. Jean is the insider / fabricated the Alison request (MEDIUM)
Jean exfils deliberately and blames a non-existent request. Her account is the last-logged-in CFO with legitimate access.
Supporting artifacts: absence of any inbound "please send roster" email in Jean's PST/OST/webmail cache; webmail (Yahoo/Hotmail/Gmail) cache showing upload of xlsx to a personal account or to the competitor forum directly; browser history hitting the competitor forum's attachment-upload endpoint; $Recycle.Bin or USN entries deleting the prompting email.
Confirms: No prompting email exists anywhere, AND browser artifacts show Jean uploading the file herself.
Rules out: Genuine inbound request email recoverable with valid Received chain.

H3. Another local account (Devon, Addison, Abijah, Sacha, Kim) used the workstation (LOW-MEDIUM)
Shared XP box; Devon last logged 2008-07-12 is within window. One of them could have copied the file via their own profile.
Supporting artifacts: NTUSER.DAT per profile — RecentDocs, Outlook MRU, TypedURLs, UserAssist; profile-scoped LNKs to m57plan.xlsx; per-user webmail cookies/cache.
Confirms: Non-Jean profile shows access to the xlsx and a webmail send/upload.
Rules out: Only Jean's NTUSER references the file.

H4. Malware / RAT exfil with no human accomplice (LOW)
Keylogger or info-stealer harvested the file. Plausible on unpatched XP, but the file ended up posted to a forum as an attachment with presumably some context, which is unusual for commodity stealers.
Supporting artifacts: Prefetch, Amcache (XP has limited), Run keys, Services, suspicious binaries in %AppData%, AV logs, anomalous svchost children.
Confirms: Persistence + C2 binary + xlsx in staging dir.
Rules out: Clean autoruns, no suspicious processes in Prefetch/registry.

H5. USB / removable-media handoff (LOW)
File copied to USB then posted from elsewhere.
Supporting artifacts: SYSTEM\MountedDevices, USBSTOR, setupapi.log, LNKs with volume serials not matching local disks, shellbags.
Confirms: USB insertion timestamps bracketing xlsx creation with LNK on removable volume.
Rules out: No removable media artifacts in window.

Top priority: resolve H1 vs H2 by reading the actual Outlook PST headers on the send of m57plan.xlsx and locating the prompting inbound email. Everything else is secondary.

Concrete next steps:
- Parse Jean's Outlook PST/OST (likely Documents and Settings\Jean\Local Settings\Application Data\Microsoft\Outlook\*.pst|ost): extract Sent Items around 2008-07, isolate the message bearing m57plan.xlsx, dump full Internet headers (PR_TRANSPORT_MESSAGE_HEADERS), record SMTP recipient address verbatim.
- Extract the inbound "Alison" request email: examine From, Reply-To, Return-Path, Received chain, Message-ID domain.
- Carve deleted items / Deleted Items folder / pst slack for any earlier version of either message.
- Pull MFT for m57plan.xlsx: birth time, $LogFile/USN entries, and any copies (m57plan*.xlsx) in temp, Outlook attachment temp (OLKxxxx), or browser cache.
- LNK and RecentDocs under Jean's NTUSER.DAT confirming she opened/saved it.
- Browser cache (IE index.dat) for webmail or competitor-forum URLs as a cross-check against H2.
- Per-profile NTUSER.DAT hives for Devon/Addison/Abijah/Sacha/Kim to rule out H3.
- Event logs (Security.evtx, though XP is sparse) for logon sessions in the send window.

Re-evaluate if: recipient address IS the real alison@m57.biz (pivots to H2/H3); no PST send record exists for the xlsx (pivots to webmail/H2); xlsx appears in another profile's MRU (pivots to H3); unknown persistence found (pivots to H4); USB volume serial appears in LNKs (pivots to H5).

inputs.user_message:
OBSERVATION:
m57plan.xlsx — a spreadsheet containing the full M57.biz employee roster (names, positions, salaries, SSNs) — was created on CFO Jean Jones's Windows XP workstation and ended up posted as an attachment in the "technical support" forum of a competitor's website; we must determine how it traveled off Jean's machine and who within M57.biz was involved.

SUPPORTING EVIDENCE:
Single-host EnCase image of Jean's WinXP workstation (10 GiB). Registered owner "Jean User". Local accounts: Administrator, Kim, Jean(last login 2008-07-20), Addison, Abijah, Devon(last login 2008-07-12), Sacha. Roster (knowns): Alison Smith (President, alison@m57.biz), Jean Jones (CFO, jean@m57.biz), Bob Blackman, Carol Canfred, Dave Daubert, Emmy Arlington, Gina Tangers, Harris Jenkins, Indy Counterching. Conflicting statements: President Alison denies ever asking for or receiving the spreadsheet; CFO Jean says Alison asked her to prepare it for the funding round and email it to her. Most M57 documents exchanged by email. No memory image, no PCAP — attribution must come from Jean's disk artifacts (email headers, PST/OST, webmail browser cache, sent items, USN journal, $Recycle.Bin, LNK/MRU).

CASE CONTEXT:
M57.biz corporate exfiltration scenario (Garfinkel/NPS). Classic twist in this scenario family: the recipient address that LOOKS like the President is actually a look-alike / spoofed external address, so Jean was socially engineered into emailing the SSN spreadsheet to an attacker-controlled inbox rather than the real alison@m57.biz. Must verify recipient address and SMTP Received chain on every send of m57plan.xlsx; do not trust From:/display name alone. Incident window ~July 2008.

entries.conclusion:
Case assessment: this is the canonical M57-Jean scenario. The likely truth is social-engineering / spoofed sender: an email purporting to be from Alison (probably alison@m57.com or similar typosquat, not @m57.biz) requested the roster; Jean complied via Outlook and attached m57plan.xlsx. The file then appeared on a competitor forum, suggesting either the attacker re-posted it or a second channel (browser webmail upload) was used. The presence of three user profiles (Administrator, Devon, Jean) and AutoAdminLogon to Administrator is a red herring worth verifying but unlikely to be the exfil vector given Jean's account had the last interactive login in the incident window.

Most likely scenarios, ranked:
1. Spoofed-sender phishing: Jean received mail from a look-alike alison@ domain, replied with the xlsx attached. Primary evidence will live in Jean's PST and IE index.dat (if she viewed the reply via OWA).
2. Webmail exfil: Jean uploaded the xlsx through a browser-based webmail (Hotmail/Yahoo/Gmail) — look for multipart POSTs in IE cache and form-data fragments.
3. Insider collusion across Devon/Administrator profile — lower probability, but Devon's last login 2008-07-12 falls inside window; rule in/out.
4. USB exfil — USBSTOR is populated, must enumerate but unlikely to explain forum posting.

Highest-yield artifacts, in order of expected payoff:
- Jean's NTUSER.DAT (RecentDocs, OpenSaveMRU, TypedURLs, ComDlg32) — proves Jean authored/opened m57plan.xlsx and shows webmail URLs typed.
- Jean's Outlook PST (Documents and Settings\Jean\Local Settings\Application Data\Microsoft\Outlook\*.pst) — the smoking-gun mail thread with full Internet headers revealing spoofed Return-Path / Received chain.
- IE index.dat for Jean (History, Cache, Cookies under Local Settings\Temporary Internet Files and Cookies) — webmail session evidence, posted form data, the actual look-alike domain.
- MFT entry + $UsnJrnl/$J for m57plan.xlsx — exact birth time, MACB sequence, any rename/copy/delete.
- LNK files and Recent\ folder for Jean — confirms which app opened the xlsx and from what path.
- Recycle Bin INFO2 under SID 1004 — deleted copies.
- Event logs (Security 528/540 logon, 552 explicit-cred) for the July 12–20 window across all profiles.
- SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Office\... MRU for Excel under each NTUSER.DAT.
- USBSTOR + setupapi.log + MountedDevices to enumerate every USB ever seen and correlate to file timestamps.

TTPs to hunt:
- T1566.002 spearphishing link / T1534 internal spearphishing (spoofed display name)
- T1114.001 local email collection (PST parsing)
- T1567.002/T1567.003 exfil over web service (webmail upload)
- T1052.001 exfil over USB (rule out)
- T1078.003 local account abuse (Administrator AutoAdmin) — check for off-hours activity

Tool sequence (disk-only case, no memory/PCAP, so adjusted to disk → enrich):
1. Build the filesystem timeline and locate the xlsx and PST first via MFT.
2. Pull all NTUSER.DAT hives (Jean, Devon, Administrator) plus SOFTWARE/SYSTEM/SAM for registry context.
3. Parse Outlook PST contents and IE index.dat for the mail and webmail traces.
4. Event log review for logon anomalies.
5. Enrich extracted spoofed domains/IPs via VT.

Cross-reference mandate: every email address, display name, and SID extracted from PST/index.dat/registry MUST be cross-checked against the M57.biz roster (Alison, Jean, Kim, Addison, Abijah, Devon, Sacha + the 9 roster names embedded in m57plan.xlsx) to flag look-alikes (alison@m57.com vs alison@m57.biz, etc.). This is a mandatory plan item, not optional.

Exhaustive collection requirements:
- Registry: SOFTWARE, SYSTEM, SAM, SECURITY from %WINDIR%\system32\config; plus NTUSER.DAT from EACH of Administrator, Devon, Jean; plus UsrClass.dat where present.
- Event logs (WinXP .evt format): AppEvent.evt, SecEvent.evt, SysEvent.evt under %WINDIR%\system32\config — all three, full window.
- Browser: IE index.dat at three locations per user — Temporary Internet Files\Content.IE5\index.dat, Cookies\index.dat, History\History.IE5\index.dat — for every user profile present.
- Webmail URL auth params to grep in index.dat and pagefile: login=, email=, user=, gausr=, Y=, T=, .src=, curmbox= (Hotmail), and Cookie: headers.
- All PST/OST files under each user's Local Settings\Application Data\Microsoft\Outlook.

Red flags that re-prioritize:
- If PST shows the request mail Return-Path != alison@m57.biz → escalate domain enrichment immediately, deprioritize USB.
- If MFT shows m57plan.xlsx born BEFORE the request mail timestamp → Jean's account of events is suspect, pivot to insider angle and pull Devon's profile in parallel.
- If IE history shows webmail POSTs to Hotmail/Yahoo with the file size matching xlsx → webmail is the exfil channel, prioritize browser artifacts over PST.
- If USBSTOR shows a device connected within minutes of xlsx LastAccess → pivot to USB exfil, pull setupapi and link2 files.
- If Administrator profile shows interactive activity in window (AutoAdminLogon makes this plausible) → expand scope to all three profiles equally.

focus_paths: Documents and Settings/Jean/Local Settings/Temporary Internet Files/Content.IE5
next_hypothesis_triggers: spoofed sender domain found in PST headers -> pivot to domain/IP VT enrichment and WHOIS
webmail POST artifact in index.dat -> prioritize browser cache carving over PST
m57plan.xlsx MFT birth time predates Alison request mail -> insider/collusion pivot, deep-dive Devon profile
USBSTOR device timestamp within 10 min of xlsx access -> pivot to USB exfil, parse setupapi.log and MountedDevices
Administrator profile shows interactive logon in window -> expand collection to Administrator NTUSER artifacts equally

inputs.user_message:
CASE:
M57-JEAN corporate exfiltration. m57plan.xlsx (M57.biz employee roster: names, salaries, SSNs) created on CFO Jean Jones's WinXP workstation and later posted to a competitor's "technical support" forum. Determine when Jean created it, how it left her machine, and who within M57.biz was involved. Conflict: President Alison denies asking for/receiving it; CFO Jean says Alison asked her to prepare and email it. Single-host EnCase image only (no memory, no PCAP). Incident window ~July 2008. Likely scenario twist: spoofed/look-alike recipient address rather than the real alison@m57.biz.

EVIDENCE AVAILABLE:
Windows XP host. ewfinfo: "Jean's hard drive from the first M57 project", examiner Donny, evidence 2008-M57-Jean, 10 GiB fixed disk, acquisition MD5 78a52b5bac78f4e711607707ac0e3f93. NTFS at sector 63. Registered owner "Jean User". SAM local accounts (all Administrators): Administrator(500), Kim(1003), Jean(1004, last login 2008-07-20), Addison(1005), Abijah(1006), Devon(1007, last login 2008-07-12), Sacha(1008); Guest disabled. SOFTWARE: WinXP, AutoAdminLogon=1, DefaultUserName=Administrator. SYSTEM: ControlSet001 current, three NIC interfaces (DHCP), USBSTOR present. Profiles on disk: Administrator, Devon, Jean. Knowns IOC patterns generated for 9 roster usernames + 9 SSNs. Outstanding: locate Jean's Outlook PST/OST, browser cache (IE index.dat / any webmail), MFT entry for m57plan.xlsx, $UsnJrnl, LNK/RecentDocs, Recycle Bin.

directives.max_depth: Verify foundational IOCs (file, profile, accounts) and execute knowns-IOC sweep across Jean's profile + pagefile + unallocated. Do not deep-parse PST/IE artifacts this batch — that is Collect work.
next_hypothesis_triggers: If m57plan.xlsx parent directory or recent MRU references a non-m57.biz domain → escalate H1 (spoofed recipient)
If knowns sweep finds a recipient address matching look-alike pattern (m57.bi, m57biz.com, rn57.biz, etc.) → escalate H1 to confirmed

If Administrator/Devon profiles show recent activity touching m57plan.xlsx → escalate H3 (other local account)

entries.conclusion:
EVIDENCE SUPPORT

The finding rests on PST mail extraction (misc.pff_export). Specific corroborating data points:

- Sent Items Message00016 ClientSubmitTime 2008-07-20 01:28:47 UTC, Sender jean@m57.biz, Subject "RE: Please send me the information now" — supports the sent event and time.
- Recipients.txt: Display "alison@m57.biz" with SMTP address "tuckgorge@gmail.com" — supports the display-name spoof and external destination.
- Attachment 1_m57biz.xls MD5 e23a4eb7f2562f53e88c9dca8b26a153 matches Desktop m57biz.xls MD5 — supports identity of exfiltrated file.
- Inbound Messages 00207/00214/00215 InternetHeaders Return-Path <simsong@xy.dreamhostps.com>, Received via xy.dreamhostps.com [208.97.188.9] (Postfix userid 558838) — supports spoof origin outside M57 infrastructure.
- "Thanks!" body content reinforces social-engineering pattern and request for secrecy.
- Roster cross-reference establishes tuckgorge has no internal mapping.

CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE

None within the cited evidence. Worth noting: a single-host disk image cannot rule out collusion fully — only that no collusion artifact appears on Jean's host. Header forgery alone doesn't preclude an insider also operating the external dreamhost account, though there is no evidence pointing that way.

ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS

1. Insider using an external dropbox-style address (tuckgorge@gmail.com) plus self-sent spoofed inbound to manufacture deniability — the disk image of Jean alone cannot exclude this for other M57 personnel. The finding's "no insider colluded" is stronger than the evidence supports; it would require examining other employees' hosts/mail.
2. Jean herself orchestrating the scheme by sending spoofed inbound from a controlled dreamhost account — not supported by any evidence, but not excluded by single-host scope.
3. The MD5 match proves file identity but not that the Desktop copy is the source vs. a copy saved from the sent attachment.

HALLUCINATION CHECK

- "208.97.188.9" and "userid 558838" — specific values; assumed accurate from header text but should be re-verified verbatim.
- "No M57 insider colluded" — overclaim. Evidence shows external delivery infrastructure and display-name spoof; it does not affirmatively clear all insiders. This is a negative finding based on single-host scope.
- "in reply to a spoofed request impersonating Alison Smith" — supported (RE: subject chain + forged From in 00214).
- ATT&CK mapping not asserted, so nothing to verify there. If mapped later: T1566.003 (Spearphishing via Service) or T1534 (Internal Spearphishing — does NOT fit since sender is external) / T1585/T1656 (Impersonation) would be appropriate; T1048 (Exfil over alt protocol) fits email exfil.

FACT-CHECK

- Postfix "userid 558838" syntax is consistent with real Postfix Received headers (from userid N) — plausible.
- 208.97.188.9 is in DreamHost's allocated range historically — plausible, but should be confirmed via enrich.vt_lookup_ip or WHOIS if attribution matters.
- MD5 e23a4eb7f2562f53e88c9dca8b26a153 — cannot independently verify the value here, but the methodology (hash comparison between attachment and Desktop file) is sound.
- NEGATIVE FINDING SCRUTINY: "No M57 insider colluded" is reached from a single host. This is a CHALLENGED negative — the disk image scope cannot prove the negative for the wider org. Recommend softening to "no evidence of insider collusion on Jean's host."

ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION

- enrich.vt_lookup_ip on 208.97.188.9 to confirm DreamHost ownership and any abuse history.
- enrich.vt_lookup_hash on e23a4eb7f2562f53e88c9dca8b26a153 for any prior sightings.
- Re-parse full InternetHeaders of 00207/00214/00215 to capture all Received hops, Message-ID domain, DKIM/SPF results if present.
- Check Jean's browser history / webmail artifacts and any local drafts for the inbound spoofed messages to rule out locally-fabricated inbound.
- ez.mftecmd on m57biz.xls and the PST to establish creation/modification timeline relative to the inbound request and the outbound send.
- If other M57 hosts become available, examine Alison's Sent Items to confirm she did not send the requests.

VERDICT: UNCERTAIN — The exfiltration event itself (Jean sent m57biz.xls to tuckgorge@gmail.com at the stated time) is SUPPORTED. The spoofing/social-engineering interpretation is well-supported by header forensics. The blanket assertion "No M57 insider colluded" is CHALLENGED because it is a negative claim derived from single-host scope; it should be scoped to "no insider-collusion evidence on Jean's host."

inputs.user_message:
FINDING:
The M57 employee roster spreadsheet (m57biz.xls — names, salaries, SSNs) was exfiltrated from Jean Jones's workstation as an Outlook email attachment sent 2008-07-20 01:28:47 UTC from jean@m57.biz to the external address tuckgorge@gmail.com, in reply to a spoofed request impersonating company president Alison Smith. No M57 insider colluded; CFO Jean was the victim of a social-engineering (phishing-for-information) attack.

SUPPORTING EVIDENCE:
misc.pff_export (call 55) of Jean's outlook.pst: Sent Items Message00016, Subject "RE: Please send me the information now", Client submit time Jul 20 2008 01:28:47 UTC, Sender jean@m57.biz, Recipients.txt shows Display name "alison@m57.biz" but Email address "tuckgorge@gmail.com" (Address type SMTP). Attachment 1_m57biz.xls present; its MD5 e23a4eb7f2562f53e88c9dca8b26a153 equals the Desktop m57biz.xls MD5 (identical file). Inbound Message00207 "background checks" From-display alison@m57.biz but InternetHeaders Return-Path <simsong@xy.dreamhostps.com>, asks Jean to build a spreadsheet of employees/salaries/SSNs and "please do not mention this to anybody". Inbound Message00214 "Please send me the information now" and Message00215 "Thanks!" both: OutlookHeaders Sender name alison@m57.biz / Sender email tuckgorge@gmail.com; InternetHeaders From: tuckgorge@gmail.com (alison@m57.biz), Return-Path <simsong@xy.dreamhostps.com>, generated "by xy.dreamhostps.com (Postfix, from userid 558838)" [208.97.188.9] — not M57 mail infrastructure. "Thanks!" body: "Thanks for the file. I'll handle it from here. Once again, please don't tell anyone about this." Cross-reference: tuckgorge@gmail.com does not match any M57 roster name or local account under case-fold/separator/initial-last normalization = external actor. Only one Sent message carried the xls.

CASE CONTEXT:
M57-JEAN corporate exfiltration. Conflict: President Alison denied asking for/receiving the spreadsheet; CFO Jean said Alison asked her to prepare and email it. Resolution: both truthful — the request emails forged Alison's identity. Hypothesis H0001-H1 (spoofed/look-alike recipient). Single-host disk image only.

entries.conclusion:
EVIDENCE SUPPORT
- Exfil event: misc.pff_export Sent Items Message00016 directly supplies sender, recipient SMTP address, client submit time, subject, and attachment name. Strong.
- Attachment identity: MD5 e23a4eb7f2562f53e88c9dca8b26a153 matching Desktop\m57biz.xls — byte-identical proof the roster left the host as that attachment. Strong.
- External origin of inbound spoofs: Return-Path simsong@xy.dreamhostps.com and Received from 208.97.188.9 in Message00207/00214/00215 InternetHeaders. Strong for "not M57 mail flow."
- Spoofing mechanism: From-display alison@m57.biz vs Sender/Reply email tuckgorge@gmail.com in OutlookHeaders/InternetHeaders. Strong.
- Roster mapping: name-normalization check that tuckgorge does not correspond to any M57 employee. Reasonable but depends on completeness of roster source — not cited.
- No tampering on host: af.timestomp_drift = 0 across full MFT. Supports absence of MFT-level timestomping only.

CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE / WEAKNESSES
- "No evidence of insider collusion on Jean's host" relies on a narrow set of checks: PST export, MFT timestomp scan, and a name-normalization match. No browser history, no LNK/jumplist, no clipboard, no registry-recent-docs, no IM/chat artifacts, no USB enumeration, no memory analysis are cited. The absence claim is under-collected even when scoped to Jean's host.
- Roster normalization match is asserted but the roster source and the normalization rules aren't enumerated in evidence. A clever alias (e.g., "tuck gorge" = nickname) cannot be excluded purely from local artifacts.
- Return-Path/Received headers can themselves be forged; trust in 208.97.188.9 as "the true origin" requires SMTP log corroboration not provided. The finding only needs "outside M57," which the headers do support, but calling 208.97.188.9 the "true origin" is slightly overclaimed.
- "Only one Sent message carried the xls" — depends on completeness of pff_export coverage (Sent Items only? Deleted Items? other PST/OST? webmail?). Not addressed.

ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- Jean could have used a secondary channel (webmail, USB, IM) in addition to email; the email exfil is proven but exclusivity is not.
- An insider could operate tuckgorge@gmail.com from off-host; this is correctly acknowledged in scope.
- simsong@xy.dreamhostps.com 208.97.188.9 could be a relay/open-host used by an insider — outside-M57 origin does not equal external actor identity.

HALLUCINATION CHECK
- "Postfix from userid 558838" — specific userid stated; presumed lifted from Received header verbatim. Acceptable if literal.
- "byte-identical" is justified by MD5 equality, technically a collision-resistance argument; fine in DFIR practice.
- No invented MITRE IDs or VAD claims. No fabricated mechanisms.
- The phrase "true origin was outside M57 infrastructure" is supported by headers but headers are attacker-controllable; mild overstatement.

FACT-CHECK
- Outlook PST Client submit time is typically UTC-stored — consistent.
- Return-Path being set by the receiving MTA from MAIL FROM is standard; an external Return-Path with M57 display From is a classic spoof pattern. Accurate.
- dreamhostps.com is a legitimate DreamHost shared hosting domain; 208.97.188.9 is in DreamHost ranges historically. Plausible, not verified here.
- ATT&CK mapping not asserted; nothing to fact-check.
- Negative finding scrutiny: the scoped negative ("no evidence of insider collusion on Jean's host") is reached primarily via PST + MFT-timestomp + roster-name match. That is not exhaustive host coverage. CHALLENGED on completeness grounds.

ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION TO UPGRADE
- ez.evtxecmd for Security/Sysmon-equivalent and Outlook MAPI traces; check for non-Outlook send paths.
- misc.regripper_hive on NTUSER.DAT: TypedURLs, RecentDocs, OpenSaveMRU, UserAssist — any browser webmail use or m57biz.xls handling outside Outlook.
- ez.appcompatcacheparser / ez.amcacheparser for execution of archivers, FTP clients, cloud sync tools.
- tsk.fls + tsk.icat over Deleted Items / additional PST/OST to confirm "only one Sent message carried the xls."
- Browser history (Places.sqlite/WebCacheV01.dat) and USB device enumeration via SYSTEM hive.
- Memory (if available) vol.netscan / vol.cmdline around 01:28 UTC to rule out concurrent exfil channels.

VERDICT: UNCERTAIN — the email exfil and external spoofing are SUPPORTED with strong evidence, but the scoped negative ("no evidence of insider collusion on Jean's host") is CHALLENGED because the supporting collection (PST + MFT timestomp + name normalization) does not exhaustively cover host-side collusion artifacts (browser, USB, registry MRUs, execution caches).

entries.conclusion:
EVIDENCE SUPPORT
- Sent message metadata (timestamp, sender, recipient SMTP, subject, attachment) from misc.pff_export of Jean's outlook.pst, Sent Items Message00016. Recipients.txt shows the spoofed display vs actual SMTP mismatch (alison@m57.biz display → tuckgorge@gmail.com SMTP).
- Attachment integrity tied to disk via MD5 e23a4eb7f2562f53e88c9dca8b26a153 matching Desktop\m57biz.xls (byte-identical claim — needs confirmation of where the disk hash was computed; not explicitly cited as a separate tool call).
- Inbound spoof evidence from Messages 00207/00214/00215: Return-Path simsong@xy.dreamhostps.com via 208.97.188.9, Postfix userid 558838, display-name spoof of alison@m57.biz.
- Host corroboration: NTUSER RecentDocs (call 99) m57biz.lnk timestamp 2008-07-20 01:28:04 (43s gap), UserAssist EXCEL/OUTLOOK, ShimCache (call 102) negative for archivers/FTP/cloud tools.

CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE
- None presented. Investigator notes no contradicting artifacts; consistent across pst, registry, and shimcache.

ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- Insider collusion: Jean could have knowingly sent to tuckgorge with the spoof email as a fabricated cover story. The forensic artifacts cannot distinguish "deceived" from "willing participant" — only that the inbound messages were spoofed and the outbound was sent via Outlook. The finding's claim that Jean was deceived (and that this resolves the Alison/Jean conflict) is an interpretive conclusion, not a forensic one.
- The Return-Path simsong@xy.dreamhostps.com could indicate the sender's actual operational infrastructure (Simson Garfinkel's dreamhost — known M57 scenario author), which is expected in this training scenario but in a real case would warrant attribution work, not assumption.
- "Only one Sent message carried the xls" — depends on whether Deleted Items, Drafts, and other PST folders, plus webmail browser artifacts, were fully enumerated. IE history/cache was checked; other browsers and non-Outlook mail clients were not explicitly cleared.

HALLUCINATION CHECK
- T1566.003 (Spearphishing via Service) typically refers to phishing via third-party services like social media/personal webmail to bypass enterprise controls. The inbound spoofed alison@m57.biz coming from dreamhost SMTP with a gmail reply target is closer to T1534 (Internal Spearphishing — if Alison were truly the sender) or T1566.002 (Spearphishing Link) / T1566.001 (Attachment) variants. The mapping to T1566.003 is debatable. T1114 (Email Collection) is misapplied — T1114 covers the adversary collecting victim email from a mail store; here the adversary tricked a user into sending a file attachment, which is more accurately T1056 social-engineering exfil or simply T1048/T1567 exfiltration over the email channel. Flag: ATT&CK mapping is overclaimed.
- "208.97.188.9" and "Postfix userid 558838" — specific values; assumed accurate from InternetHeaders parse but not independently verified in the evidence excerpt.
- "byte-identical" attachment claim requires a separate hash of the disk file; not explicitly shown as a separate tool call in the evidence (assumed from MD5 match statement).
- The conflict-resolution conclusion ("both truthful") is investigative reasoning, not a forensic fact derivable from artifacts.

FACT-CHECK
- T1566.003 Spearphishing via Service: MITRE describes this as adversary using third-party services (LinkedIn, personal webmail) rather than corporate email. The inbound came through Postfix on dreamhost claiming to be alison@m57.biz — this is spoofed corporate email, which aligns more with generic T1566 or T1534 patterns. CHALLENGED.
- T1114 Email Collection: refers to harvesting email content from mailboxes/servers. Not applicable here. CHALLENGED.
- correlate.mitre_validate is not in the approved tool list provided. Flag.
- Negative findings (no archiver/FTP/cloud, no webmail upload): ShimCache and IE history are partial. ShimCache only records executed binaries that meet certain criteria; portable tools or script-based exfil may not appear. Other browsers (Firefox, Chrome) not addressed. Prefetch, Amcache, and BITS were not cited.

ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION
- ez.amcacheparser and ez.appcompatcacheparser to confirm no archiver/uploader executed.
- ez.mftecmd / tsk.fls for Firefox/Chrome profile directories and any other mail client artifacts.
- ez.evtxecmd for Outlook MAPI events or any process creation around 01:28:04–01:28:47.
- misc.pff_export over Deleted Items and Drafts folders of the PST to confirm uniqueness of the exfil send.
- Re-map ATT&CK: consider T1566 (generic) or T1534 for the inbound spoof; T1048/T1567 or no exfil sub-technique for the outbound.

VERDICT: UNCERTAIN — The factual chain (spoofed inbound → Jean's Outlook send → xls attached → external gmail) is well-supported by PST artifacts and host corroboration. However, the ATT&CK mappings (T1566.003, T1114) are incorrect for the observed behaviour, and the "both truthful" conclusion is interpretive, not forensic. Core narrative SUPPORTED; technique IDs and intent attribution CHALLENGED.

entries.conclusion:
Analysis follows.

1. EVIDENCE SUPPORT
- Send event: misc.pff_export of outlook.pst, Sent Items Message00016 — Subject, ClientSubmitTime 2008-07-20 01:28:47.828 UTC, Sender jean@m57.biz, Recipient SMTP address tuckgorge@gmail.com with display "alison@m57.biz", Attachment 1_m57biz.xls. Directly supports the send, time, sender, recipient mismatch, and attachment presence.
- Byte-identity: hash.hash_file on both extracted attachment and Desktop\m57biz.xls returning identical MD5/SHA1/SHA256 and size 291840. Directly supports byte-identical claim.
- Inbound spoof characterization: Messages 00207/00214/00215 show display From alison@m57.biz, actual sender tuckgorge@gmail.com, Return-Path simsong@xy.dreamhostps.com, Received via 208.97.188.9. Supports the inbound display/envelope divergence.
- RecentDocs timestamp 2008-07-20 01:28:04 (43 seconds before send) corroborates the file was opened immediately prior.

2. CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE
- None presented. No conflicting Sent Items entries, no alternate hash, no other outbound message carrying the xls.

3. ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- The "spoofing" characterization of inbound mail is the only soft spot. Return-Path simsong@xy.dreamhostps.com via 208.97.188.9 (DreamHost) is consistent with a legitimately-sent message from a Gmail-aliased or DreamHost-hosted account where the display name was simply set to "alison@m57.biz" — i.e., display-name impersonation rather than envelope forgery. The finding wording "forged and did not originate from M57 mail infrastructure" is technically accurate (it didn't come from m57.biz), but "forged" should be read as display-name deception, not SMTP envelope forgery. The evidence supports the former.
- The send itself has no benign alternative that survives scrutiny: Jean's PST, Jean's account, attachment on Jean's desktop, opened 43s prior. That is a Jean-workstation-originated send. (Intent is correctly excluded per scope.)

4. HALLUCINATION CHECK
- "via 208.97.188.9" — cited as Received header value, sourced from InternetHeaders of Message00207. OK if the raw header was actually surfaced in the pff_export output; assumed present.
- "byte-identical" — fully supported by triple-hash + size match. Solid.
- "Only one Sent message carried the xls" — stated in supporting evidence as a collection-scope conclusion. This is a mild negative-finding claim. It relies on completeness of the pff_export enumeration of Sent Items and attachments. Acceptable if all Sent Items were enumerated (pff_export typically does). Worth a one-line confirmation that the enumeration was exhaustive across all PST folders (not just Sent Items — e.g., Drafts, Outbox, custom folders).
- No invented ATT&CK IDs, no invented offsets, no fabricated mechanism. Clean.

5. FACT-CHECK
- SMTP/Outlook semantics correct: PST Recipients table stores SMTP address separately from display name; the address-type SMTP with value tuckgorge@gmail.com is the authoritative envelope recipient. Correct.
- DreamHost IP 208.97.188.9 is within DreamHost's known range (208.97.128.0/18). Plausible and consistent with xy.dreamhostps.com hostname.
- Hash values are syntactically valid MD5/SHA1/SHA256 lengths. Not independently verifiable here but internally consistent.
- Negative-finding scrutiny: "Only one Sent message carried the xls" — single-tool (pff_export) pass. Adequate if folder coverage was full; flag as MINOR if not confirmed.

6. ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION
- Confirm pff_export enumerated all PST folders (Drafts, Outbox, Deleted Items, custom) for any other instance of m57biz.xls or related attachments — run misc.pff_export with full folder listing or grep all Attachment entries.
- enrich.vt_lookup_hash on SHA256 34456b5f714dc9d8dd23c742d54c3f5f582ecb042bc1c4d3042b88203863779f to see prior sightings (low priority — not needed to support this finding, useful for broader case).
- ez.evtxecmd for any Outlook/MAPI send events around 01:28:47 UTC as secondary corroboration.
- Pull the full Internet headers of Message00016 (the outbound) to confirm whether it was actually transmitted via M57 SMTP infrastructure vs. Outlook-only artifact.

7. VERDICT: SUPPORTED — Each factual claim is directly tied to named tool output fields. The finding is appropriately scoped (no intent, no ATT&CK). Only a minor caveat: "forged" inbound mail is better characterized as display-name impersonation with external envelope; the wording is defensible but should be read precisely. Negative claim ("only one Sent message") rests on a single pff_export pass — exhaustive folder coverage should be confirmed.

entries.conclusion:
Analysis:

1. EVIDENCE SUPPORT
- Author/Company/Dates: exiftool output directly supports OLE metadata claims (Author "Alison Smith", LMB "Jean User", Company "M57.BIZ", Create 2008-06-12 15:13:51, Modify 2008-07-20 01:28:03).
- Content tokens: strings -e l confirmed "Salary", surname tokens, "Alison" present in workbook.
- File size 291,840 bytes & MFT $SI Created 2008-07-20 01:28:03.656: mftecmd inode 32712.
- RecentDocs LNK target opened 2008-07-20 01:28:04: NTUSER hive.
- Timestomp drift 0: af.timestomp_drift on MFT.
All major factual claims are directly cited.

2. CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE
- Minor: finding says "written/last-opened 2008-07-20 01:28:03–04". MFT $SI Created = 01:28:03.656 is cited but $SI Modified/Accessed not shown. Strictly the "written" claim relies on Created; LastWrite not in the excerpt. Weak but not contradicting.
- The surname "Counterching" is unusual — should verify spelling is from the actual worksheet, not OCR/strings artifact. This name does not match a known common surname pattern; could be a strings false-positive concatenation.

3. ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- Create date 2008-06-12 reflects template origin, not Jean authoring on that date. The finding correctly notes this. "When did Jean create this spreadsheet" — Jean did NOT create it; she received/saved it. Author Alison Smith. This nuance is preserved.
- $SI Created 2008-07-20 could reflect file copy/save-as from email attachment rather than authoring — consistent with exfil scenario but should be stated.

4. HALLUCINATION CHECK
- "immediately before the 01:28:47 send" — the 01:28:47 send timestamp is not in the supporting evidence block; it's referenced as context. Acceptable if established in prior finding, otherwise unsourced.
- "$SI and NTUSER RecentDocs" both cited — supported.
- No invented offsets or fabricated mechanisms.

5. FACT-CHECK
- exiftool OLE property extraction is standard and reliable for XLS BIFF streams.
- MFT $SI semantics correct.
- RecentDocs in NTUSER\...\RecentDocs is correct registry location.
- af.timestomp_drift = 0 supports authenticity of MFT timestamps (compares $SI vs $FN).
- File size 291,840 bytes = ~285 KB, exiftool reports "292 kB" (decimal kB), consistent.

6. ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION
- Pull $FN timestamps from mftecmd to corroborate $SI Created and confirm no timestomp at file level (not just aggregate drift).
- Verify "Counterching" token by extracting the BIFF SST (shared strings) with oledump or ssview rather than raw strings, to confirm it's a real cell value not a concatenation artifact.
- Cross-reference the 01:28:47 send timestamp (pcap/SMTP) in a citable evidence row.

7. VERDICT: SUPPORTED — Document identity, metadata, and Desktop save timestamp are well-evidenced. Minor caveat: the "01:28:47 send" timing should be cross-cited, and one worksheet token ("Counterching") deserves SST-level confirmation.

entries.conclusion:
Analysis follows.

1. EVIDENCE SUPPORT
- DreamHost relay for the SSN-request thread: misc.pff_export call 55 shows Received from 208.97.188.9 with Return-Path simsong@xy.dreamhostps.com on Messages 00207/00214/00215, and the actual sender field is tuckgorge@gmail.com. VT/ASN attribution to New Dream Network is reasonable but the supporting evidence cites "VT ASN 26347" without showing the enrich.vt_lookup_ip output explicitly — accept as plausible but note it's a summary not a quoted excerpt.
- AT&T residential IP for the "alex" decoy: Messages 00206/00209/00213 Received "from jean13fbf038a3 (unknown [70.134.85.172])". AbuseIPDB attribution cited but not quoted — minor gap.
- Jean's verification question and reply: Sent Items Message00010 and Message00017 are cited verbatim. Strong.
- Host scope of m57biz.xls handling: NTUSER RecentDocs (call 99) shows only Excel open at 01:28:04; UserAssist enumerates Office/browser/IM/games with no archiver/FTP/cloud; ShimCache (call 102) negative for WinRAR/7z/FileZilla/PuTTY/Dropbox; IE history benign. Reasonable coverage for the insider-tooling hypothesis on this host.

2. CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE
- The "alex@m57.biz" persona originates from an AT&T residential IP — that IP could plausibly belong to an M57 employee working from home. The finding acknowledges this caveat but the headline "external social-engineering attack" understates it. 70.134.85.172 needs WHOIS geo correlation against known M57 employee residences before being declared external with confidence.
- The hostname "jean13fbf038a3" in the Received header is suspicious — it resembles Jean's own machine naming convention. This could indicate (a) header forgery designed to look like Jean's host, (b) coincidence, or (c) something more interesting. Not addressed.

3. ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- An M57 employee operating tuckgorge@gmail.com from home over AT&T residential and routing through DreamHost is fully consistent with the same evidence. The finding's own last sentence concedes this but the opening sentence ("external social-engineering attack, not an M57 insider action") overstates.
- "Spoofed Alison" could also be Alison herself using an external account for deniability — host evidence on Alison's machine would be needed to refute.

4. HALLUCINATION CHECK
- "VT ASN 26347 New Dream Network/DreamHost" — ASN number is asserted but the supporting evidence does not include a quoted enrich.vt_lookup_ip excerpt. Plausible (DreamHost is ASN 26347 in reality) but flagged as not directly cited.
- "AT&T per abuseipdb" — provider attribution asserted without quoted lookup excerpt.
- "tuckgorge@gmail.com matches no M57 roster name/local account under normalization" — the normalization procedure and the roster source are not described. How was the roster obtained from a single-host image? If it's from the leaked xls itself, say so.

5. FACT-CHECK
- ASN 26347 = New Dream Network is correct in the real world.
- 70.134.x.x is in AT&T/SBC space — consistent.
- Display-name spoofing via SMTP is technically trivial and matches the described pattern.
- Negative-finding scrutiny: the claim "Outlook was the sole channel that touched m57biz.xls" rests on RecentDocs + UserAssist + ShimCache + IE history. This is decent but not exhaustive — missing: $MFT timeline around 01:28:04 for the xls, prefetch, jumplists, LNK files, USN journal, $LogFile, Firefox history/forms (UserAssist shows firefox.exe ran), and Firefox upload artifacts. IE was checked but Firefox usage is acknowledged and Firefox places.sqlite/formhistory were not cited. This is a material gap for the "no webmail upload" claim.
- "Single-host scope cannot affirmatively clear other M57 personnel" — correctly hedged.

6. ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION

entries.conclusion:
EVIDENCE SUPPORT

The finding rests on Outlook PST extraction (misc.pff_export) which directly shows:
- Jean's verification question and the alex@m57.biz reply
- The "Whoops" message with Received header from 70.134.85.172 with HELO jean13fbf038a3
- The exfil thread routed via 208.97.188.9 (DreamHost) with actual sender tuckgorge@gmail.com
- The LNK machine ID matching the HELO string

Negative findings are supported by NTUSER RecentDocs, UserAssist, ShimCache, IE history/cache, Firefox places/downloads/formhistory.

CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE

The HELO jean13fbf038a3 matching Jean's machine name is unusual and ambiguous. Two readings:
1. Attacker spoofed HELO using a string they harvested (e.g., from a prior email header, LNK metadata leaked in attachments, or social engineering).
2. The mail actually originated from Jean's machine via an outbound relay through 70.134.85.172 — which would contradict the "external attacker" framing.

The finding does not adequately address option 2. AT&T residential IP 70.134.85.172 being unrelated to Jean's home connection is asserted but not proven (no geolocation cross-check against Jean's known residence/ISP shown).

ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS

- Jean herself could have sent the decoy messages from a secondary device on her home network (the HELO match is suspicious in this direction).
- Another M57 employee with knowledge of Jean's machine name (visible in shared LNK metadata, network browsing, AD) could be operating tuckgorge@gmail.com — finding correctly acknowledges this.
- Compromise of Jean's machine by malware that sent mail externally — not ruled out (no malfind/process scan cited).

HALLUCINATION CHECK

- "display-name spoof" — supported by the alex/alison header inconsistency in misc.pff_export. OK.
- "DreamHost 208.97.188.9" with ASN 26347 — supported by enrich.vt_lookup_ip. OK.
- "AT&T residential IP 70.134.85.172" — IP appears in Received header per evidence, but the "AT&T residential" characterization is not backed by a cited enrich.vt_lookup_ip call in the evidence. Minor unverified specificity.
- "LNK tracker machine ID jean-13fbf038a3 == HELO jean13fbf038a3" — supported by call 49. OK, though the hyphen difference is glossed.
- Negative findings — coverage is reasonable across the cited artifacts but does not include process memory analysis or USB device history (USBSTOR registry keys, setupapi logs) to rule out USB exfil device insertion. ShimCache absence of archiver tools is not equivalent to absence of USB exfil.

FACT-CHECK

- Attribution is properly hedged to "scoped to Jean's host" — good.
- "Intent is an inference, not a forensic fact" — appropriate hedge.
- Negative claim "no webmail upload" rests only on Firefox/IE artifacts. If a third browser, portable browser, or non-browser HTTP tool were used, this would be missed. No process list / netscan equivalent for the disk image is cited.
- USB exfil rule-out is missing: no USBSTOR, no setupapi.dev.log, no shellbag check for removable drives cited. ShimCache alone does not cover this.
- The HELO/machine-name correspondence is presented as supporting external-attacker theory but is equally consistent with origination from Jean's machine. The finding underweights this.

ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION

- enrich.vt_lookup_ip on 70.134.85.172 to confirm AT&T residential attribution and geolocation
- misc.regripper_hive on SYSTEM hive for USBSTOR/MountedDevices to rule out USB exfil
- ez.evtxecmd / event logs for outbound SMTP or unusual network service activity near the email send timestamps
- Check setupapi.dev.log and shellbags for removable media
- Verify the LNK machine-ID hyphenation and whether jean13fbf038a3 (no hyphen) is the actual NetBIOS name vs LNK GUID-derived ID

VERDICT: UNCERTAIN — the Outlook header evidence and channel-exclusivity findings are solid, but the HELO-matches-hostname observation cuts both ways and the USB/removable-media exfil vector is not exhaustively excluded.

If event logs show outbound SMTP service on Jean's host, the HELO match supports local origination not spoof

entries.conclusion:
Analysis of the DFIR findings against the evidence tier standard:

LOGICAL GAPS

G1 (ADVISORY): The transport mechanism from USB to Desktop is not directly evidenced. F3 places a USB connection 1m45s before the Desktop write, but no shellbag, LNK file, jumplist, or MFT parent-directory evidence ties the xls origin to the USB volume. The roster's prior provenance (Author "Alison Smith", Created 2008-06-12) is not reconciled with how it reached Jean's Desktop on 2008-07-20. This is acknowledged implicitly in H5 ("partial") but should be explicit.

G2 (ADVISORY): The HELO string "jean13fbf038a3" appearing in the decoy thread (F6) is not mechanistically explained. No investigation into whether this hostname is discoverable externally (DNS, prior mail headers leaked, LinkedIn, etc.) — which would distinguish "attacker knew the hostname" from "mail originated locally." This gap is material to H2/H6.

CONTRADICTIONS

None of substance. F1 and F6 are coherent (F1 = proven send; F6 = ambiguous priming thread).

TIER VIOLATIONS

V1 (BLOCKER): F6 is labeled SUSPECTED but the cited evidence is genuinely ambiguous between two opposing mechanisms (HELO spoofing vs. local origination). Per the tier standard, a single ambiguous indicator with no corroborating artifact is UNCONFIRMED, not SUSPECTED. SUSPECTED requires a behavioral indicator or single corroborating point pointing in one direction; here the evidence is bidirectional. Recommend re-tier to UNCONFIRMED, or split into two findings each tiered separately.

V2 (ADVISORY): F3 tier is appropriate (SUSPECTED — timing correlation is a behavioral indicator), but the prose "Candidate transport/source" should be qualified to make clear no volume serial, no LNK MAC, and no shellbag tie the xls to that device.

TIER CONTRADICTIONS

None detected. No two findings make the same mechanistic claim at different tiers.

OVERCLAIMED MECHANISMS

O1 (ADVISORY): F1 states "inbound identity forged, not from M57 infrastructure" — supported by Return-Path/IP. Solid. No overclaim.

O2 (ADVISORY): The H2 conclusion ("Jean witting insider not supported") is correctly scoped to host-bounded, but the phrasing "verification challenge + intact private thread" is presented as exculpatory evidence. These are consistent with both a duped Jean and a witting Jean creating cover. Recommend report language reflect that absence of cover-up artifacts ≠ evidence of non-collusion; intent is simply out of scope.

MISSING INVESTIGATION

M1 (BLOCKER — EVIDENCE EXHAUSTION): USB artifact category appears under-exhausted given F3 is the only candidate non-email transport. Specifically not cited: setupapi.dev.log full parse, MountPoints2 under NTUSER, EMDMgmt (ReadyBoost) for volume serial, LNK files in Recent/Office Recent referencing a removable volume letter, shellbags for the USB volume, and MFT $LogFile/$UsnJrnl for the xls creation event showing source path. Without these, F3 cannot move from SUSPECTED toward LIKELY or be conclusively excluded.

M2 (BLOCKER — EVIDENCE EXHAUSTION): Process execution evidence for the send event (2008-07-20 01:28:47Z ±) not cited. Amcache, RecentFileCache.bcf (n/a XP), Prefetch (OUTLOOK.EXE, EXCEL.EXE timestamps and run counts), and the .pf files around 01:28 would corroborate OUTLOOK was the sending process and EXCEL opened the file from where. Prefetch on XP is high-value and not mentioned in coverage.

M3 (ADVISORY): VSS / restore points on XP (System Volume Information\_restore{...}) not mentioned. Could contain earlier PST states or registry hives showing earlier USB insertions or earlier xls copies.

M4 (ADVISORY): No mention of checking whether the inbound priming messages (forging alison@m57.biz) had any prior delivery to Jean before 2008-07-20 — i.e., reconnaissance/grooming timeline in the PST Inbox and Deleted Items. Relevant to attributing the social-engineering sequence.

M5 (ADVISORY): Firefox formhistory entry "jean@m57.com" (lookalike) is noted but not pivoted on — when was it typed, on what page (URL), and is there a corresponding places.sqlite visit? This bears on F6 and on whether Jean was interacting with a phishing page.

M6 (ADVISORY): Cross-reference of the external sender tuckgorge@gmail.com against any suspect list / case-provided persona list is not stated. If the case context provided a suspect roster (competitors, former employees), exhaustion requires that cross-check be explicit.

Analysis of the finding:

The finding is a final synthesis answer for the well-known M57-Jean scenario. Let me work through each component.

1. EVIDENCE SUPPORT
- Email exfil event: misc.pff_export call 55 reportedly shows Sent Message00016 from jean@m57.biz to tuckgorge@gmail.com at 2008-07-20 01:28:47 UTC with attachment 1_m57biz.xls. SHA256 match (34456b5f...) to Desktop\m57biz.xls supports byte-identical attachment claim.
- Forgery infrastructure: Return-Path simsong@xy.dreamhostps.com via 208.97.188.9 cited from inbound messages 00207/00214/00215. DreamHost ASN 26347 is verifiable WHOIS.
- Author metadata: exiftool call 47 — OLE Author "Alison Smith", LastModBy "Jean User", created 2008-06-12; Desktop file mtime 2008-07-20 01:28:03 (precedes send by 44s — plausible attach-then-send).
- Negative findings: browser history checked (IE + Firefox); no webmail upload artifacts; no insider tooling.

2. CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE
- None apparent from the supplied evidence. The 44-second gap between Desktop file mtime and send timestamp is internally consistent.

3. ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- "Deceived rather than witting": this is an inference. A witting insider could deliberately mimic deception by replying to a spoofed thread she herself solicited or coordinated out-of-band. Host-only artifacts cannot distinguish "deceived" from "colluding with plausible deniability." The finding correctly scopes this as "indicates" and "inference," which is appropriate.
- The alex@m57.biz "decoy" could be a legitimate-but-compromised internal account rather than an external persona; the evidence cited doesn't prove alex is external/forged (only alison is explicitly tied to DreamHost Return-Path).

4. HALLUCINATION CHECK
- SHA256 truncated to "34456b5f..." — partial hash is fine as long as full hash exists in tool output. Acceptable.
- ASN 26347 for DreamHost — verifiable, correct.
- IP 208.97.188.9 attributed to DreamHost — consistent with DreamHost's 208.97.0.0/17 range. Plausible.
- Claim that alex-decoy is a "decoy persona" — supporting evidence says "alex-decoy reply reassured her" but does not state that alex@m57.biz was verified as forged/external in the same way alison was. This is a softer claim than presented.
- "No other M57 employee is implicated" — supported only by absence on this single host; explicitly scoped, acceptable.

5. FACT-CHECK
- Negative findings (no webmail/forum exfil) — supported by IE + Firefox coverage. Reasonable but should confirm Chrome/Safari/other browsers absent on host, and that pagefile/unallocated were swept.
- Forum posting attribution as "downstream" is appropriately scoped to off-host.
- The forgery mechanics (Return-Path vs From header mismatch) are technically correct email-spoofing tradecraft.
- Intent attribution ("deceived") is correctly labeled as an inference and scoped to host evidence.

6. ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION
- Verify alex@m57.biz header provenance (Return-Path, Received chain) via misc.pff_export on the alex thread — currently the finding asserts decoy status without citing the same level of header analysis used for alison.
- Confirm browser sweep exhaustiveness: any Chrome/Opera profiles, pagefile.sys carving for HTTP POST remnants, $MFT for transient upload temp files.
- Confirm no USB/removable media exfil via setupapi.dev.log and USBSTOR registry keys.
- Confirm no cloud-sync clients (Dropbox, etc.) installed via Amcache/AppCompatCache.

7. VERDICT: SUPPORTED (with one soft spot)
The core chain — Jean sent the file to tuckgorge@gmail.com at 01:28:47 UTC with byte-identical attachment, in a thread where alison@m57.biz From-header was forged via DreamHost — is well-evidenced. The "alex decoy" sub-claim is less rigorously supported in the cited evidence than the alison forgery. Intent is properly hedged. Forum/off-host scoping is correct.
